title: SMR Construction Schedule: A Reality Check for Canada's BWRX-300 | Ramesh (Ram) Budwani, CCE/A, CCA, F.A.B.I posted on the topic | LinkedIn
text:
I recall Leading SMR Suppliers saying "32-36" Months for the Construction Schedule. Vs. Realistically speaking suggest Look at the Scheduled/Reported Completion/COD of the SMR (Fleet of 4 Units) under-construction in Canada (BWRX-300). Granted this is a F-O-A-K SMR and should be a Shorter Time Period for the N-O-A-K. (EVEN for the 4th Unit Vs. the 1st Unit) Btw: The Critical Path for the SMR should be along the lines of Typical Large Size Nuclear Plants. P.S: In my opinion, the Estimated Major "Bill of Materials/BOQs" seems to one ofthe Important "Metrics" with Potential bearing on the Construction Schedule. Unless I missed it NO Leading SMR Suppliers has Shared/Reported any Data Related to the "BOQs". #DATABASE(1970-2025)
Hard truth: Every SMR technology provider I've reviewed has "wildly optimistic" schedules. A couple of years ago, I was asked to review project delivery schedules for multiple SMR technology providers. Since no SMR data existed, we benchmarked against recent Gen III & Gen III+ reactors and developed criteria for FOAK SMR implementation. The result? SMR schedules were consistently unrealistic, Particularly in design/licensing and construction phases. Meanwhile, as we know large plant delivery is going in the opposite direction - massive schedule and cost overruns. Justifications given: -> "Regulators are familiar with our systems & components"….maybe -> "Factory modular construction saves time compared to stick-build"…..ok. Is it going to require a complete paradigm shift to deliver SMRs at pace and on budget? I think the answer is YES…..but we need to look beyond nuclear for solutions. What if we treated SMR deployment like tech companies approach product development? Instead of just digital twins for plant design, what if we digitally modelled the ENTIRE project - enabling scenario testing and risk analysis before issues hit? Cross-sector approaches that might help transform SMR delivery: 1. Spotify "Squad Model" -> Traditional nuclear teams are organized by system but lack decision authority. Spotify squads have bounded autonomy within pre-defined safety envelopes - they can optimize efficiency, routing, maintenance access without upward approval, but safety-significant changes still require formal review. 2. Tesla "Over-the-Air Updates" -> Design SMRs with software-updateable components so FOAK operational data automatically triggers NOAK design improvements. 3. SpaceX "First Principles Manufacturing" -> Challenge every assumption about nuclear component production. Why do reactor internals take months to manufacture? Could 3D printing reduce delivery from months to weeks? 4. Netflix "Chaos Engineering" -> Deliberately introduce controlled "failures" in digital twin to identify vulnerabilities before building physical systems. 5. Apple "Design Thinking" -> Design from operator perspective first - intuitive control rooms, streamlined maintenance, error-resistant workflows. 6. Google "Site Reliability Engineering" -> Embed reliability engineers in design teams accountable for NOAK operational performance from day one. Key insight: technology transfer is 20% technical, 80% cultural adaptation. Are we being honest about SMR delivery timelines- or setting up next round of nuclear disappointments? Political will and regulatory support are improving, but without changing how we deliver, do we risk repeating old mistakes on SMRs? Which cross-sector approaches would derisk SMR schedules - and what other industries should nuclear be learning from? Are current SMR schedules realistic, or do we need a paradigm shift? #SMR #Nuclear #ProjectDelivery #Innovation #DigitalTransformation #Technology #Leadership
